common_name: State Fair Zinnia (Tall Classic Large-Flowered Mixed Zinnia)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun; State Fair is among the tallest zinnia varieties (30–36 inches) and produces sturdy stems for cutting. In zone 9, direct sow March–May for summer bloom or August for fall. Stake if necessary in exposed windy sites. State Fair zinnia petals are thick and durable — the flowers hold up in the garden through rain and heat better than thinner-petaled varieties.

directions: Sow seeds 0.25 inch deep after last frost; thin to 12–18 inches. State Fair is a classic large-flowered zinnia mix producing very tall plants (30–42 inches) with large double flowers (4–5 inches) on stems long enough for impressive cut flower arrangements. State Fair represents the traditional American zinnia ideal — the tall, robust, large-flowered zinnia that was grown in American gardens for decades as the primary summer cutting flower before hybrid varieties introduced more compact, uniform types. The flowers come in the full zinnia color range: scarlet, rose, salmon, orange, gold, cream, lavender, white, and bicolors — the mixed seedling planting creating a naturalistic, abundant, old-fashioned garden scene. The thick petals and sturdy stems make State Fair excellent for cutting at the bud-opening stage for 7–10 day vase life. Plants bloom prolifically from midsummer through frost with regular harvesting or deadheading. The tall habit (30–42 inches) distinguishes State Fair from compact modern types and suits it for the back of borders, cutting garden rows, and locations where height is desired.
sources: Burpee Seeds — State Fair zinnia variety notes
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Classic tall zinnia description
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Cut-flower zinnia variety comparison

common_name: Dragon Wing Begonia (AAS-Winning Heat-Tolerant Pendulous Annual Begonia)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or purchased transplants after last frost; Dragon Wing Begonia is typically sold as finished plants from nurseries (the standard series is vegetatively propagated). Plant in partial shade to full sun — one of the most heat-tolerant begonias available. In zone 9, Dragon Wing performs throughout the hot summer in areas with morning sun and afternoon shade, where standard impatiens fail from high temperatures. Space 12–18 inches for containers and beds; the cascading habit makes Dragon Wing excellent in hanging baskets.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in fertile, well-drained soil or potting mix after last frost; provide regular moisture and monthly fertilizer. Dragon Wing Begonia received AAS recognition in 2000 (Dragon Wing Red) for exceptional heat tolerance combined with large, pendulous, continuously-blooming performance — two attributes that distinguish it markedly from standard wax begonias (B. semperflorens). Standard wax begonias are compact, mound-forming plants with small flowers (0.5–1 inch) that tolerate heat reasonably well but become stressed in extended high temperatures. Dragon Wing grows as an open, somewhat pendulous plant with large, glossy, dark green, wing-shaped leaves and clusters of large (1.5–2 inch) pendulous flowers in bright red or pink that hang in elegant, semi-cascading clusters. The pendulous, open habit makes Dragon Wing exceptional in containers and hanging baskets where the flowering branches spill attractively over the edge. The extreme heat tolerance — Dragon Wing continues to bloom vigorously in temperatures that shut down impatiens and stress standard begonias — makes it one of the most reliable summer shade container plants for the Gulf South. Dragon Wing performs acceptably in full sun in the Gulf South when moisture is consistent, but performs optimally in partial shade.
sources: All-America Selections — Dragon Wing Red begonia AAS winner 2000
Ball Horticultural — Dragon Wing begonia series introduction
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Begonia Performance in the Gulf South

common_name: Easy Wave Petunia (Seed-Raised Trailing Spreading Annual Petunia)

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting; petunia seed is tiny and requires light for germination — sow on the surface without covering and keep at 70°F under lights. Easy Wave petunias are more compact than the original Wave series and better suited to containers and hanging baskets. In zone 9, transplant February–March for spring bloom; a fall planting in September–October produces excellent winter blooms. Easy Wave tolerates more heat than standard petunias and continues to bloom in conditions that stress standard types.

directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist, sterile starting mix under bright light (no covering — needs light); maintain at 70°F until germination. Transplant carefully after last frost. Easy Wave is a seed-raised version of the famous Wave petunia breeding line from PanAmerican Seed/Ball Horticultural — developed to bring the spreading, trailing habit and continuous bloom of the vegetatively-produced Wave series into a form that home gardeners can grow from seed. The original Wave petunias (Purple Wave, AAS 1995) are vegetatively propagated cuttings sold as finished plants; Easy Wave produces very similar performance from seed. The spreading, trailing habit distinguishes Wave-type petunias from standard petunias: instead of growing as a compact mound, Easy Wave spreads outward (18–24 inch spread) and slightly cascades, creating the 'wave' of color for which the series is named. Easy Wave petunias are low-maintenance — they self-clean (fallen petals drop cleanly), are more heat-tolerant than standard petunias, and bloom continuously without deadheading. The spreading habit makes them exceptional as groundcovers, in hanging baskets where the trailing branches spill over the edge, and in containers where they fill and overflow attractively.
sources: PanAmerican Seed / Ball Horticultural — Easy Wave petunia series introduction
All-America Selections — Wave petunia AAS recognition history
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Trailing petunia variety comparison

common_name: Plumed Celosia (Feathery Plume Cockscomb Summer Annual)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is consistently above 65°F; celosia resents root disturbance and direct sowing usually outperforms transplants. In zone 9, plant April–June for summer and fall bloom. Celosia thrives in heat and humidity — it is one of the summer annuals that blooms more vigorously (not less) in the Gulf South's hot, humid conditions, unlike many other annuals that slow down in summer.

directions: Sow seeds 0.125 inch deep after last frost; thin to 8–14 inches. Plumed celosia is a variety group of Celosia argentea producing feathery, upright, flame-like flower plumes rather than the flat, brain-shaped crests of the cockscomb (var. cristata) type. The plumes are dense, velvety, and upright, ranging from 4–8 inches tall, in brilliant colors: deep scarlet, crimson, orange, salmon, yellow, cream, and hot pink — hot colors that intensify in summer heat. Celosia is one of the most heat-tolerant summer annuals available, thriving in conditions that cause most other plants to retreat — the combination of intense heat, humidity, and high soil temperatures that characterize Gulf South summers promotes vigorous blooming rather than reducing it. The long-lasting cut flowers are prized in both fresh and dried arrangements: the plumes hold their color and shape well when dried, making them valuable for dried flower crafts and fall arrangements. Young leaves and flowers are edible and used as cooked greens in West African and Southeast Asian cooking, where celosia is grown as a vegetable crop. Garden series include Kimono, Fresh Look, and Intenz, each offering improved uniformity and specific color ranges.
sources: All-America Selections — Celosia plumosa series AAS awards (multiple)
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers for the Gulf South
Burpee Seeds — Plumed celosia variety descriptions

common_name: Napoletano Basil (Italian Large-Leaf Lettuce-Leaf Neapolitan Basil)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting after last frost; Napoletano's very large leaves (sometimes 4–6 inches across) require slightly more space than smaller-leafed basil types. In zone 9, transplant March–April; a second planting in August–September provides fall production before the first frost. Napoletano is the traditional basil of Neapolitan pizza — large leaves torn and placed on pizza just before serving, where the heat wilts them without cooking, preserving the aromatic character.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; transplant to full sun in rich, well-drained soil after last frost. Pinch flower buds as they appear to maintain leaf production. Napoletano (also called Italian Large-Leaf or Lettuce Leaf Basil) is the traditional basil of Neapolitan cuisine — a variety with exceptionally large, broadly oval, slightly crinkled, aromatic leaves that can reach 4–6 inches across in good growing conditions, two to three times the size of standard Genovese basil leaves. The very large leaf size makes Napoletano particularly well-suited to certain traditional Italian preparations: the leaves are large enough to use whole as a pizza topping without chopping, attractive as a garnish or salad base, and produce impressive yields of leaf area per harvest. The flavor is rich, sweet, and classically basil — virtually identical to Genovese in fragrance and taste, as the large leaf size is a morphological rather than a chemical difference between the varieties. Napoletano performs well in zone 9's warm summers and is one of the basil varieties that handles Gulf South heat and humidity acceptably, though like all basil it bolts in response to day length and hot temperatures — regular pinching extends productive life.
sources: Franchi Sementi (Italy) — Napoletano basil historical documentation
Seed Savers Exchange — Italian heirloom basil collection
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Basil Production and Variety Guide
